elements.sent-2411-001: We use moment-SOS (Sum Of Squares) relaxations to address the optimal control problem of the 1D heat equation perturbed with a nonlinear term.
elements.sent-2411-002: We extend the current framework of moment-based optimal control of PDEs to consider a quadratic cost on the control.
elements.sent-2411-003: We develop a new method to extract a nonlinear controller from approximate moments of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-004: The control law acts on the boundary of the domain and depends on the solution over the whole domain.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method is validated numerically and compared to a linear-quadratic controller.
elements.sent-2411-006: The control of nonlinear Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) presents additional challenges over the linear case, due to the inherent complexity and potential for chaotic behavior in these systems.
elements.sent-2411-007: Several contemporary problems in fluid dynamics \cite{control-navier-stokes , as well as in fluid-structure interaction (\cite{fsi-1,num-method-fsi ), require advanced control techniques to successfully drive the solution to the desired target state.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: A first strategy to tackle that kind of problem is to consider linear control techniques such as the Linear-Quadratic Regulator (LQR).
elements.sent-2411-009: In the case of linear PDEs, it provides the optimal control law at the continuous level of the problem (see \cite{course-LQR for instance) which precedes any domain discretization, as well as in the finite-dimensional formulation that usually follows a PDE approximation scheme (e.g., Finite Difference/Element/Volume methods).}
elements.sent-2411-010: One classic approach to extend the LQR to the class of nonlinear PDEs is to derive the controller from the linearized equation and apply it to the initial nonlinear problem \cite{riccati-proj .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This method requires the use of specific numerical techniques involving fine discretization of space and time \cite{num-method-fsi .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, LQR control laws are only efficient for initial states that are sufficiently close to the target.
elements.sent-2411-013: This implies that there exists a region of initial states in the solution space outside of which a LQR controller cannot drive the system towards the target.
elements.sent-2411-014: We illustrate this situation later in Section \ref{sec:nonlinear_case .}
elements.sent-2411-015: More advanced methods consist in constructing nonlinear controllers for the control problem of nonlinear PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the literature, these methods can be divided into two main categories.
elements.sent-2411-017: The first one gathers the approaches that construct a nonlinear control law from the continuous formulation of the model and then discretize the system.
elements.sent-2411-018: We shall denote these methods by "Model-Control-Discretize", see \cite[Section 17.12]{quarteroni-optim-then-discrete .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Among these, the backstepping method \cite{backstepping-ref is particularly noteworthy for transforming the original system into a more manageable form for stabilization.}
elements.sent-2411-020: It is based on control lyapunov functions \cite{coron-CLF that ensure the stability of the target system.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, finding an appropriate control lyapunov function is often a difficult task because there is no systematic way to construct such functions, especially for strongly nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-022: The process can be highly problem-specific and may require significant mathematical insight.
elements.sent-2411-023: Similarly, Koopman-based dynamic mode decomposition approaches (e.g., \cite{DMD-ref,peitz2019koopman ) use a specific change of variables, the Koopman transform, which converts the original nonlinear PDE into a linear PDE in a new set of variables.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Again, the main challenge of the method relies in finding such a Koopman transform.
elements.sent-2411-025: The second major category comprises the "Model-Discretize-Control" methods.
elements.sent-2411-026: Notable approaches within this family include methods based on Pontryagin's maximum principle \cite{PMP-ref .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Other techniques solve the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation \cite{HJB-2 or state-dependent Riccati equations \cite{SDRE-ref to derive a stabilizing control law.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These approaches tackle high-dimensional problems and often require long computation time to converge.
elements.sent-2411-029: Recent advancements in machine learning control \cite{machine-learning provide a viable and innovative alternative to the methods discussed above.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We consider in this paper a moment-based approach that relies on the concept of occupation measures \cite{KHL-2018 , which allows to relax the problem of control of nonlinear PDEs into a Linear Program (LP) in the space of Borel measures.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The main interest of this framework is its overall convexity and its versatility, as it covers a wide variety of polynomial PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-032: The method falls into the category of "Model-Control-Discretize" approaches as it operates directly on the continuous formulation of the PDE control problem, thus avoiding the need for spatio-temporal discretization.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this advantage comes at the expense of having to solve a sequence of convex semidefinite programming problems, or Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMI) of increasing size.
elements.sent-2411-034: This approach has already been applied to the 1D Burgers' equation, first to extract a stabilizing distributed control \cite{KHL-2018 , and later to derive the entropy solutions for Riemann problems \cite{moment-entropy-PDEs,moment-entropy-param-dependent .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: An SOS method dual to the moment method is followed in \cite{moment-integral-variational-1 and \cite{moment-integral-variational-2 to find lower bounds on integral variational problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: For optimal control of linear PDEs, \cite{EDP-magron proposes to approximate measures on infinite-dimensional vector spaces with measures on finite-dimensional vector spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Measures supported on infinite-dimensional vector spaces are considered in \cite{moment-inf-dim and are used to solve numerically the heat equation perturbed with a distributed quadratic nonlinearity.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The contributions of this article are threefold: First, we extend the framework of \cite{KHL-2018 to incorporate a quadratic cost on the control, similar to the LQR.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Second, we develop a new method for reconstructing a boundary control in feedback form, based on the solution over the entire domain.
elements.sent-2411-040: Third, we validate our method on a practical case where we consider the boundary control problem of the 1D heat equation perturbed with linear and nonlinear terms.
elements.sent-2411-041: Fig. \ref{fig:steps_extract_controller outlines the main steps of our method of resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-042: To the best of the authors’ knowledge, the problem of control of 1D semilinear heat equations has not yet been addressed using the moments of occupation measures supported on finite-dimensional spaces.
elements.sent-2411-043: It will serve as a possible starting point for further studies on harder, higher-dimensional control problems, such as 2D semilinear heat equations, fluid-structure interaction problems, etc..
elements.sent-2411-044: The remainder of this article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \ref{sec:section2 presents the PDE control problem that we consider and our contribution to extend the framework of \cite{KHL-2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section \ref{sec:section3 discusses the validation of our method applied to a class of 1D semilinear heat equations.}
elements.sent-2411-047: We demonstrate a case where the LQR approach derived from the linearized PDE cannot control the system towards the equilibrium, whereas the moment-based approach can.
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, Section \ref{sec:conclusion concludes the paper and outlines future research perspectives.}

elements.sent-2411-052: We consider the problem of control of the following 1D semilinear heat equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:heat1D} \begin{dcases} \frac{\partial y}{\partial x_1}(x) = \lambda\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x_2^2}(x) + \alpha y(x) + \eta y(x)^3, \quad \forall x\in \Omega, y(x) = 0, \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_W, y(x) = u(x), \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_E, y(x) = y_0(x), \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_I, \end{dcases}\end{equation} where $y \in W^{2,\infty (\Omega)$ is the unknown scalar function, $u \in L^2(0,1)$ is the control, $y_0 \in L^2(0,1)$ is a polynomial initial condition, $\lambda > 0$ and $\alpha, \eta \geq 0$ are fixed parameters.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:heat1D} \begin{dcases} \frac{\partial y}{\partial x_1}(x) = \lambda\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x_2^2}(x) + \alpha y(x) + \eta y(x)^3, \quad \forall x\in \Omega, y(x) = 0, \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_W, y(x) = u(x), \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_E, y(x) = y_0(x), \quad \forall x \in \partial \Omega_I, \end{dcases}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-053: We are interested in the problem of minimizing a functional $\mathcal{L}$ over the set of pairs $(y,u)$ subject to \eqref{eq:heat1D .}
elements.sent-2411-054: We consider a quadratic cost function of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:cost_function} \mathcal{L}(y,u) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{\Omega} y(x)^2 \ud x + \frac{R}{2} \int_{0}^1 u(x_1)^2 \ud x_1,\end{equation} where $R>0$ is a fixed parameter.
elements.sent-2411-055: The optimal control problem of $\eqref{eq:heat1D}$ finally writes \begin{equation}\label{eq:optim_control_prblm} \begin{array}{ll} \underset{\scriptstyle \text{$(y,u) \in W^{2,\infty}(\Omega) \times L^2(0,1),$}}{\inf} \mathcal{L}(y,u), \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\text{s.t.} \text{$(y,u)$ subject to \eqref{eq:heat1D}}. \end{array}\end{equation} We denote by $\ell_{\inf}$ the infimum of \eqref{eq:optim_control_prblm .}
elements.sent-2411-056: We apply the nonlinear optimal control techniques developed in \cite{KHL-2018 to solve this problem.}
